title: Fatayer bil sabanekh
subtitle: Lebanese spinach and sumac pastry parcels
excerpt: Yeasted dough parcels filled with spinach, onion, sumac, and lemon, folded into triangles and baked until the edges are golden and the filling is fragrant. A classic Lebanese bakery item eaten for breakfast or as a snack, served warm or at room temperature. The sumac provides a fruity tartness that cuts through the richness of the dough.
sourceNotes: Fatayer (also spelled ftayer) are Lebanese stuffed pastry parcels documented in Anissa Helou's 'Lebanese Cuisine' (1994) as a bakery standard. Three fillings are traditional: spinach with sumac, cheese with mint, and minced meat. The spinach version is the most widely eaten. Fatayer are traditionally baked on a very hot surface for a short time, producing a soft dough with a slightly firm base.

content.text: The filling must be thoroughly drained before using. Raw spinach releases a large amount of water as it wilts, and a wet filling soaks through the dough and makes it impossible to seal. Salt the spinach, leave for 10 minutes, then squeeze in a cloth until completely dry. The filling should look like a compact, dark green mass with no visible liquid.

Fatayer are shaped by placing a ball of filling in the centre of a dough circle and pulling three sides up to meet in a point at the top, pinching the seams firmly. The triangle shape is traditional. If the seams are not well sealed, they will open during baking, pinch repeatedly along each seam and press firmly.
Mix the {{plain-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} together. Add the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} and 175 ml warm water. Knead for 8 minutes until smooth. Cover and rest for 1 hour.

Toss the chopped {{spinach}} with half a teaspoon of salt and leave for 10 minutes. Squeeze firmly in a cloth to remove all moisture. Mix with the {{onion}}, {{sumac}}, {{lemon}} juice, {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}}, and {{pine-nuts}} if using. Taste and adjust seasoning, the filling should be well seasoned and clearly tart from the sumac.

Heat the oven to 220°C fan with baking trays inside. Divide the dough into 12 equal pieces. Roll each into a circle about 12 cm across. Place a heaped teaspoon of filling in the centre. Pull three sides of the circle up to meet at the top, pinching the seams firmly to form a triangle. Carefully slide onto the hot trays. Bake for 12 to 15 minutes until golden at the edges. Eat warm or at room temperature.

items.fix: Squeeze all moisture from the spinach. Pinch each seam repeatedly along its entire length, pressing the dough together firmly. A slightly damp finger can help the dough adhere at the seams
items.cause: Filling too wet or seams not firmly pinched

items.fix: The squeezing step removes the moisture that would otherwise steam the pastry from inside. The filling should be almost crumbly, if it drips, squeeze more
Pre-heating the baking tray is essential for a firm, lightly coloured base. Place the trays in the oven while it preheats and transfer the shaped fatayer directly onto the hot surface
items.cause: Tray not pre-heated or oven too low

items.fix: Taste the raw filling before shaping. It should be assertively seasoned with sumac and lemon, since the flavour mellows considerably once enclosed in the dough

content.text: Fatayer are the Lebanese bakery item, made in large quantities in neighbourhood bakeries from early morning and carried home in paper bags for breakfast. They occupy the same role in Lebanese daily life that croissants do in French life: a pastry that requires some skill to make at home but that is so central to the food culture that many people know how to make it. The spinach-sumac version is the most common. In Lebanon they are also made with a za'atar and olive oil filling (the same as manakish but enclosed), and with a cheese and mint filling. Fatayer appear on the table at every mezze spread alongside hummus, labneh, and kibbeh.
